Q: Can you share any personal tips, tricks, or 'hacks' you use when planning a complex trip?
A: Great, I can see your screen.
Imagine you're living on the Sunshine Coast in Queensland, and you're planning a holiday to London.
Start by telling us what you would consider first when planning a long-haul trip like this.
You can approach this however you normally would.
Show us where you start, what you prioritize, and how you move through the planning process.
You might look at flights, explore different travel dates, Compare routes and stopovers, or check multiple websites to see how prices vary?
There are no right or wrong answers.
We're interested in your planning process, the tools you prefer to use, and how you compare and evaluate your options.
I'm going to open the website for you now.
Please remember to think aloud as you work through the task, and let me know when you feel you've completed it.

We just finished looking at the flights to London.
Now I'd like to switch gears a bit and talk about how you approach planning a multi-city international trip.
To start, when you're planning such a trip, what's the very first decision you usually make?

The very first decision I make if it's a multi-trip international trip, multi-city, is I think about what the order is of the cities I'm going to go to, where to first, and what's the sequence of flights going to look like.
Screener Q: That's interesting.
So, the order of cities and the flight sequence are your initial focus.
Can you walk me through how you decide on that order?
[Speaker:LISA FRANCIS] Yeah, I can give an example.
I went on a trip where I was going to both Tokyo and London.
So, it was a multi-city trip and then returned to Melbourne.
And what I had to think about was, was I going to London first and then Tokyo?
Going to Tokyo and back to Melbourne, or was I going Tokyo first, then London, and then Melbourne?
So I think about the order of the cities and the flight sequence first before I start researching flights on our website.
[Speaker:Thinking Machines] Thank you for that example.
That helps clarify things.
So when faced with a choice like London, then Tokyo, or Tokyo, then London, what factors do you consider to decide which city comes first?
Q: I just think about what I feel more comfortable with.
I might look up how the difference in price of the flights might differ depending on what sequence I might do.
I'll also think about whether I might be meeting any friends or I want to be in a particular city for a particular date.
And I also think about how it might fit in with my accommodation plans.
Q: It sounds like you weigh several factors.
Can you give me a specific example of a time when the price difference of flights influenced your decision on the order of cities?

[Speaker:LISA FRANCIS] Yeah, sometimes when I've done a multi-city trip, if I've gone in a different sequence, the price of the flights is considerably different, and I am a value shopper, so I do tend to look for the lower cost option.

I do like to get flights that are a good price, so yes, it is influential in my decision that if I can reorder the cities and get better priced flights, that's something I'll certainly weigh into when I'm booking my flights.
[Speaker:ASKABLE x Thinking Machines] To start, when you're planning a trip, how do you decide which websites or platforms you'll use to begin your research?
[Speaker:LISA FRANCIS] I've got a few websites that I use that I find are really good.
I use the Webjet the Jet website a lot.
I find that's a really good website.
I go direct to airline websites like Qantas, Virgin, and Jetstar.
I've used the Expedia website before, find that helpful.
For accommodation, I've used Booking.com.
I've also used WhatIf.com.
So I guess I've got a number of websites that I've used before for planning trips, and I tend to go to them because I know that they are good websites for researching holiday options.
[Speaker:ASKABLE] You mentioned you tend to go to these websites because you know they are good.
What specifically makes a website good for researching holiday options in your experience?
[Speaker:LISA FRANCIS] What makes a website good is if on that particular website I can find flights or accommodation for the city that I'm looking to travel to.
Also, what makes a website good is that I can find either flights or hotel accommodation that look like they're good value for money and not too expensive.
And yeah, a good website is where I can find what I'm looking for, which is basically flights or hotel accommodation for the places that I'm looking to travel to.
Also, when you're looking for— oh, one other thing that makes a website good is one that's easy to navigate and straightforward to navigate.
[Speaker:ASKABLE x Thinking Machines] That's helpful.
So ease of navigation is important.
When you're looking for flights or accommodation and trying to determine if something is good value for money, what information or features on a website help you make that assessment?
[Speaker:LISA FRANCIS] I look on a website if I varied my dates, whether the price of the flights will vary significantly, and I'll also compare the price on a few different websites.
So I might look at flights on, say, for example, Webjet, and then look for the same flights on Expedia and see if they differ significantly or if there's not much difference.
I'll also—
[Speaker:ASKABLE x Thinking Machines] That's a smart strategy, comparing across multiple sites.
Beyond just the price, are there any other pieces of information or features on a flight or accommodation listing that help you determine if it's a good value?
For example, do details about luggage, layovers, or cancellation policies play a role?
